section.title: Israel Forsakes God
section.sectionRationale: This is the first great oracle of the book's opening movement, the covenant lawsuit proper. Having been commissioned in chapter 1, Jeremiah now brings the LORD's case against His people: a bride's early devotion traded for worthless idols, a fountain forsaken for broken cisterns, and a divorced woman's shameless return to lovers who have already ruined her.
section.thesis: The LORD recalls Israel's bridal devotion in the wilderness, indicts her for forsaking Him, the fountain of living water, for broken cisterns of her own making, and exposes the folly of a people who deny their guilt even as their own history condemns them.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the devotion of Israel's youth remembered; the covenant lawsuit and the forsaken fountain; the relentless chase after other lords; a universal, brazen harlotry; and the searching question, would you now return to Me.

units.label: Remembered devotion
units.note: the love of a bride in the wilderness

verses.text: Now the word of the LORD came to me, saying,

“Go and proclaim in the hearing of Jerusalem that this is what the LORD says: 
‘I remember the devotion of your youth, your love as a bride, how you followed Me in the wilderness, in a land not sown.
Israel was holy to the LORD, the firstfruits of His harvest. All who devoured her found themselves guilty; disaster came upon them,’” declares the LORD.

units.label: The covenant lawsuit
units.note: the fountain forsaken, broken cisterns dug

verses.text: Hear the word of the LORD, O house of Jacob, and all you families of the house of Israel.

This is what the LORD says: 
“What fault did your fathers find in Me that they strayed so far from Me, and followed worthless idols, and became worthless themselves?

They did not ask, ‘Where is the LORD who brought us up from the land of Egypt, who led us through the wilderness, through a land of deserts and pits, a land of drought and darkness, a land where no one travels and no one lives?’
I brought you into a fertile land to eat its fruit and bounty, but you came and defiled My land, and made My inheritance detestable.
The priests did not ask, ‘Where is the LORD?’ The experts in the law no longer knew Me, and the leaders rebelled against Me. The prophets prophesied by Baal and followed useless idols.
Therefore, I will contend with you again, declares the LORD, and I will bring a case against your children's children.
Cross over to the coasts of Cyprus and take a look; send to Kedar and consider carefully; see if there has ever been anything like this:
Has a nation ever changed its gods, though they are no gods at all? Yet My people have exchanged their Glory for useless idols.
Be stunned by this, O heavens; be shocked and utterly appalled,” declares the LORD.

“For My people have committed two evils: 
They have {{azab|forsaken}} Me, the fountain of living water, and they have dug their own {{bor|cisterns}}, broken cisterns that cannot hold water.

units.label: Chasing after other lords
units.note: prey, a wild donkey, an unashamed thief

verses.text:
Is Israel a slave? Was he born into slavery? 
Why then has he become prey?
The young lions have roared at him; they have growled with a loud voice. They have laid waste his land; his cities lie in ruins, without inhabitant.
The men of Memphis and Tahpanhes have shaved the crown of your head.

Have you not brought this on yourself by forsaking the LORD your God 
when He led you in the way?
Now what will you gain on your way to Egypt to drink the waters of the Nile? What will you gain on your way to Assyria to drink the waters of the Euphrates?

Your own evil will discipline you; your own apostasies will reprimand you. Consider and realize how evil and bitter it is for you to forsake the LORD your God and to have no fear of Me,” declares the Lord GOD of Hosts.

“For long ago you broke your yoke and tore off your chains, 
saying, ‘I will not serve!’ Indeed, on every high hill and under every green tree 
you lay down as a prostitute.
I had planted you like a choice vine from the very best seed. How could you turn yourself before Me into a rotten, wild vine?
Although you wash with lye and use an abundance of soap, the stain of your guilt is still before Me,” declares the Lord GOD.
“How can you say, ‘I am not defiled; I have not run after the Baals’? Look at your behavior in the valley; acknowledge what you have done. You are a swift young she-camel galloping here and there,

a wild donkey at home in the wilderness, sniffing the wind in the heat of her desire. 
Who can restrain her passion? All who seek her need not weary themselves; in mating season they will find her.

You should have kept your feet from going bare and your throat from being thirsty. But you said, ‘It is hopeless! For I love foreign gods, 
and I must go after them.’
As the thief is ashamed when he is caught, so the house of Israel is disgraced. They, their kings, their officials, their priests, and their prophets
say to a tree, ‘You are my father,’ and to a stone, ‘You gave me birth.’ For they have turned their backs to Me and not their faces, yet in the time of trouble they beg, ‘Rise up and save us!’

But where are the gods you made for yourselves? Let them rise up in your time of trouble 
and save you if they can; for your gods are as numerous as your cities, O Judah.
Why do you bring a case against Me? You have all rebelled against Me,” declares the LORD.
“I have struck your sons in vain; they accepted no discipline. Your own sword has devoured your prophets like a voracious lion.”
Does a maiden forget her jewelry or a bride her wedding sash? Yet My people have forgotten Me for days without number.

How skillfully you pursue love! Even the most immoral of women 
could learn from your ways.

Moreover, your skirts are stained with the blood of the innocent poor, 
though you did not find them breaking in. 
But in spite of all these things

you say, ‘I am innocent. 
Surely His anger will turn from me.’ Behold, I will judge you, because you say, ‘I have not sinned.’
How unstable you are, constantly changing your ways! You will be disappointed by Egypt just as you were by Assyria.
Moreover, you will leave that place with your hands on your head, for the LORD has rejected those you trust; you will not prosper by their help.”

units.label: Can she return?
units.note: the brazen look of a prostitute, yet still His question

verses.text: “If a man divorces his wife and she leaves him to marry another, can he ever return to her? Would not such a land be completely defiled? But you have played the harlot with many lovers, and you would {{shuv|return}} to Me?” declares the LORD.

“Lift up your eyes to the barren heights and see. Is there any place where you have not been violated? You sat beside the highways waiting for your lovers, like a nomad in the desert. You have defiled the land with your prostitution and wickedness.
Therefore the showers have been withheld, and no spring rains have fallen. Yet you have the brazen look of a prostitute; you refuse to be ashamed.
Have you not just called to Me, ‘My Father, You are my friend from youth.
Will He be angry forever? Will He be indignant to the end?’ This you have spoken, but you keep doing all the evil you can.”

azab.gloss: To forsake, abandon, leave behind. It is the covenant word for abandoning a relationship of loyalty and love; Israel's core sin is named here not primarily as breaking rules but as forsaking a Person, the fountain of living water Himself.

bor.gloss: A pit or cistern dug to store rainwater, as opposed to a spring or fountain of flowing water. A broken cistern cannot hold what is poured into it, a vivid picture of every substitute for God that promises life and cannot deliver it.

shuv.gloss: To turn, return, repent. This is Jeremiah's key word for genuine covenant repentance, a physical and relational turning back to the LORD, used with piercing irony here of a people who ask to return without any intention of turning from their evil ways.

basic.context: God calls Jeremiah to remind Jerusalem of an earlier love: how Israel followed Him through the wilderness like a devoted young bride. But that memory only sharpens the charge that follows: God's people have forsaken Him, chased other gods with an appetite Jeremiah compares to a wild animal in heat, and now dare to ask whether God will simply take them back without any real change.
basic.hermeneutics: This is poetic covenant lawsuit language, using marriage and legal courtroom imagery that was familiar in the ancient Near East for describing a broken relationship of loyalty. The hardest thing to sit with honestly is how vivid and unflattering the imagery becomes, God is not softening the charge to spare feelings; the severity of the language matches the seriousness of forsaking the living God for what cannot save.
basic.originalLanguages: Israel's sin is named as azab, forsaking, the covenant word for abandoning a relationship, and specifically forsaking the fountain of living water for broken bor, cisterns, that cannot hold water. By 3:1 the people ask to shuv, return, but Jeremiah's whole book will insist that shuv means an actual turning of life and worship, not merely words.
basic.typology: The forsaken fountain of living water anticipates Jesus' claim to be that very fountain, offering the woman at the well water that becomes in her a spring welling up to eternal life, John 4:14. Where broken cisterns fail every generation, Christ is presented as the fountain that never runs dry and never disappoints.
basic.christ: Jesus directly takes up this image in John 4:10-14 and 7:37-38, presenting Himself as the living water Israel forsook here, the only source that actually satisfies the thirst broken cisterns cannot. Where Israel's history is one of restless searching after substitutes, Christ offers Himself as the end of that search.
basic.god: God is the wounded husband of this covenant, whose anger is not cold rejection but the grief of a real relationship betrayed; He remembers the devotion of Israel's youth even while indicting her present unfaithfulness. His justice names sin honestly, yet His willingness to still ask, in Jeremiah 3, whether she will return shows a mercy that keeps the door open even now.
basic.crossReferences: John 4:10-14 has Jesus offer the living water Israel forsook. John 7:37-38 repeats the invitation publicly at the feast. Hosea 2:14-20 develops the same marriage imagery of a wife pursued despite unfaithfulness. Revelation 21:6 promises the fountain of the water of life freely given at last in the new creation.
basic.oneStory: This is Israel's covenant lawsuit, the diagnosis behind the disaster that will unfold across the rest of Jeremiah: a people who traded the living fountain for cisterns that cannot hold water. The whole story of Scripture answers this failure with Christ, the true and inexhaustible fountain, finally offered freely to all who thirst.

context.literary: This oracle takes the form of a rib, a covenant lawsuit, drawing on ancient Near Eastern treaty language in which a suzerain formally charges a vassal with breach of covenant, complete with a call to witnesses, verse 12, be stunned, O heavens. The marriage metaphor, begun with tender memory in 2:2 and ending with brazen shamelessness by 3:3, structures the entire unit as a relationship's collapse.
context.historical: Delivered early in Jeremiah's ministry, likely under Josiah before his reforms had fully taken hold, this oracle addresses generations of accumulated idolatry, alliances with Egypt and Assyria sought instead of trust in the LORD, and worship practices, high places, sacred trees, that had corrupted Israelite religion for centuries.
context.canonical: This lawsuit form echoes Deuteronomy's covenant curses and Hosea's marriage imagery, and it sets the theological baseline, forsaking the fountain for broken cisterns, that explains why judgment must fall in the chapters ahead, while planting the question of return that will surface again and again through the book.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To confront Judah with the full weight of her covenant unfaithfulness by contrasting an idealized memory of devotion with the present reality of idolatry, so that the coming judgment is understood as just rather than arbitrary, while still holding open the possibility of genuine return.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage describes Israel's specific historical unfaithfulness using vivid marital and legal metaphors; it is not prescribing marriage practices or divorce law. The enduring truth it teaches is that forsaking God for lesser sources of life is always self-destructive, and that God takes covenant loyalty with the seriousness of a wounded spouse.

debates.question: Does Jeremiah 3:1's citation of the divorce law from Deuteronomy 24 mean God has formally and irrevocably divorced Israel?

views.label: A rhetorical question exposing shameless presumption
views.case: Jeremiah 3:1 reads as a rhetorical question, given the logic of the divorce law, how could such a woman expect to return, exposing how brazen it is for Israel to expect reconciliation without repentance, not announcing an actual legal divorce.

views.label: A real statement of ruptured covenant standing
views.case: Some read this as declaring that Israel's unfaithfulness really has severed the covenant relationship in a formal sense, paralleling the certificate of divorce language used of the northern kingdom in 3:8, making the offer to return all the more remarkable as grace beyond what the law would permit.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that any return is possible only by God's undeserved mercy, not by Israel's legal right to reconciliation, which is precisely why chapter 3 will go on to invite what strict justice would not require.

study.originalLanguages: Israel's sin is defined as azab, forsaking, the covenant term for abandoning a relationship of loyalty, compounded by digging broken bor, cisterns, that cannot hold water in place of the LORD, the fountain of living water. By 3:1, the people ask to shuv, return, but the verb's full meaning, a genuine turning of life and worship back to God, will be tested throughout the rest of the book against merely verbal requests for restoration.

typology.type: The fountain of living water forsaken for broken cisterns, Jeremiah 2:13
typology.fulfillment: Christ's offer of living water to the woman at the well and at the feast, John 4:10-14 and 7:37-38, the true, unfailing fountain Israel had rejected.

study.christ: Jesus deliberately picks up Jeremiah's imagery, offering Himself as the living water this passage says Israel forsook, water that becomes in the believer a spring welling up to eternal life, John 4:14, and publicly inviting the thirsty to come and drink, John 7:37-38. Where broken cisterns leave every generation of idol-worshippers empty, Christ presents Himself as the source that never runs dry, fulfilling exactly what this indictment says Israel lost.
study.god: God speaks here as a grieving covenant husband, remembering real devotion in Israel's youth even as He indicts her present unfaithfulness with unflinching honesty. His justice will not pretend the offense is small, yet even amid the charge, He is already the God who asks whether she will return, holding mercy open even before repentance is offered.

crossReferences.note: Jesus offers the Samaritan woman living water, directly answering the forsaken fountain of Jeremiah 2:13.
Jesus publicly invites anyone thirsty to come to Him and drink, the living water Israel forsook now freely offered.
Develops the same marriage metaphor of a wandering wife pursued and ultimately restored by a faithful husband.
The fountain of the water of life is finally given freely in the new creation, resolving what Israel forsook here.

study.oneStory: This is Israel's covenant lawsuit, God's formal charge that His people traded the fountain of living water for cisterns that cannot hold it. It is the diagnosis behind everything that follows in Jeremiah, and the whole of Scripture answers this ancient failure in Christ, the true fountain finally opened for all who will come and drink.

quiz.q: What does God remember about Israel at the start of this oracle?

options.text: The devotion of her youth, her love as a bride following Him through the wilderness.
options.note: Verse 2 opens with this tender memory before the charge of unfaithfulness follows.

options.text: That Israel never once obeyed Him.
options.note: The opening memory is explicitly one of devotion, not perpetual disobedience.

options.text: That Israel built the temple faithfully.
options.note: The passage recalls wilderness devotion, not the later temple building.

options.text: That Israel always trusted foreign alliances.
options.note: Trust in foreign alliances is condemned later in the chapter, not part of the remembered devotion.

quiz.q: What are the two evils God names in Jeremiah 2:13?

options.text: Forsaking the fountain of living water and digging broken cisterns that cannot hold water.
options.note: This is the central indictment of the whole oracle, named explicitly in verse 13.

options.text: Failing to pay tithes and neglecting the Sabbath.
options.note: The two evils named here are forsaking God and pursuing worthless substitutes, not these specific practices.

options.text: Building too many cities and too few farms.
options.note: This is not the concern of the passage at all.

options.text: Trusting priests instead of prophets.
options.note: The passage criticizes priests and prophets alike, but the named evils are forsaking God and broken cisterns.

quiz.q: How does the passage describe Israel's pursuit of other gods?

options.text: Like a wild donkey in heat, whose passion cannot be restrained.
options.note: Verse 24 uses this graphic image to show the restless, uncontrollable nature of Israel's idolatry.

options.text: As a rare and occasional lapse.
options.note: The passage describes persistent, relentless, and shameless pursuit, not rare lapses.

options.text: As something forced upon Israel against her will.
options.note: The text presents Israel as an eager, willing pursuer, not a victim forced into idolatry.

options.text: As a quiet, private matter with no public consequence.
options.note: The passage explicitly ties Israel's idolatry to public disaster and shame, not private, consequence-free behavior.

quiz.q: What ironic question does Israel ask in Jeremiah 3:4-5, and how does God respond to it in the passage?

options.text: She calls God 'My Father, my friend from youth' while still doing all the evil she can.
options.note: The irony is stark: comfortable religious language paired with unchanged behavior.

options.text: She sincerely repents and God immediately restores her.
options.note: The passage shows words without a matching change of life, not genuine repentance.

options.text: She denies knowing God at all.
options.note: She actually claims a relationship with God using familiar language, not denial of knowing Him.

options.text: She asks for a new king instead of God's forgiveness.
options.note: Her words concern her relationship with God directly, not a request for a king.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament pick up the image of the forsaken fountain in Jeremiah 2:13?

options.text: Jesus offers Himself as living water to the woman at the well and to the crowds at the feast.
options.note: John 4:10-14 and 7:37-38 present Christ as the true, unfailing fountain Israel had forsaken.

options.text: It has no connection anywhere in the New Testament.
options.note: Jesus explicitly uses living water imagery that echoes this very passage.

options.text: It is only referenced in the book of Revelation.
options.note: Revelation 21:6 does pick it up, but so do John 4 and John 7, making this an understatement.

options.text: It refers only to literal baptism practices in the early church.
options.note: The imagery is about Christ as the source of spiritual life, not primarily a reference to baptismal practice.

questions: God remembers Israel's early devotion even while indicting her present unfaithfulness. What season of closeness to God do you look back on, and what has changed since then?
The core charge is forsaking the fountain of living water for broken cisterns. What broken cisterns, things that promise life but cannot deliver it, are you still digging?
Israel's pursuit of other gods is described as restless and unrestrained. Where in your life does that same restlessness show up, always chasing the next thing rather than resting in God?
Israel could say, 'My Father, my friend from youth,' while still doing all the evil she could. Where might your own words about God have gotten ahead of any real change in how you live?
Even after naming Israel's guilt so honestly, God still asks whether she will return. What would it look like for you to actually turn back this week, not just to say the words, but to shuv?

translationNotes.note: Living water translates mayim chayyim, water that flows and moves, as opposed to stagnant water collected in a cistern; the contrast between a flowing spring and a static, cracked pit is central to the image.
The valley likely refers to the Valley of Hinnom, associated elsewhere in Jeremiah with child sacrifice to Molech, making this an especially damning accusation rather than a vague reference.

This verse alludes directly to the divorce law of Deuteronomy 24:1-4, which forbade a man from remarrying a wife he had divorced after she had married another; Jeremiah uses that legal background to expose how presumptuous it is for Israel to expect an easy return.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the Makor scripture toolkit this session.
